corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: In this work, we introduce a simple framework for con- trastive learning of visual representations, which we call SimCLR .

context_window.previous: Discriminative approaches based on contrastive learning in the latent space have recently shown great promise, achieving state-of-the- art results (Hadsell et al., 2006; Dosovitskiy et al., 2014; Oord et al., 2018; Bachman et al., 2019).
context_window.current: In this work, we introduce a simple framework for con- trastive learning of visual representations, which we call SimCLR .
context_window.next: Not only does SimCLR outperform previous work (Figure 1), but it is also simpler, requiring neither special- ized architectures (Bachman et al., 2019; Hénaff et al., 2019) nor a memory bank (Wu et al., 2018; Tian et al., 2019; He et al., 2019; Misra & van der Maaten, 2019).

location.sentence: The Contrastive Learning Framework Inspired by recent contrastive learning algorithms (see Sec- tion 7 for an overview), SimCLR learns representations by maximizing agreement between differently augmented views of the same data example via a contrastive loss in the latent space.

top_candidates.sentence: Beyond linear evaluation, we also compare against state-of-the-art on semi-supervised and transfer learning.

Furthermore, we find that attaching the linear classifier on top of the base encoder (with a stop_gradient on the input to linear classifier to prevent the label information from influencing the encoder) and train them simultaneously during the pretraining achieves similar performance.

As illustrated in Figure , this framework comprises the following four major components. • A stochastic data augmentation module that transforms any given data example randomly resulting in two correlated views of the same example, denoted xi and xj , which we consider as a positive pair.
Our framework allows various choices of the network architecture without any constraints.
Our framework allows various choices of the network architecture without any constraints.

Algorithm 1 SimCLR's main learning algorithm. input: batch size N , constant τ , structure of f , g, T . for sampled minibatch {x k } N k=1 do for all k ∈ {1, . . . , N } do draw two augmentation functions t ∼ T , t ∼ T # the first augmentation # projection end for for all i ∈ {1, . . . , 2N } and j ∈ {1, . . . , 2N } do update networks f and g to minimize L end for return encoder network f (•), and throw away g(•) Algorithm 1 summarizes the proposed method.

While similar findings hold for supervised learning , we find the gap between supervised models and linear classifiers trained on unsupervised models shrinks as the model size increases, suggesting that unsupervised learning benefits more from bigger models than its supervised counterpart.
As we have noted in the main text, most individual components of SimCLR have appeared in previous work, and the improved performance is a result of a combination of these design choices.
Table C.1 provides a high-level comparison of the design choices of our method with those of previous methods.
A high-level comparison of design choices and training setup (for best result on ImageNet) for each method.

In our framework, we decouple the prediction task and encoder architecture, by random cropping (with resizing) and using the final representations of two augmented views for prediction, so we can use standard and more powerful ResNets.

In our framework, we decouple the prediction task and encoder architecture, by random cropping (with resizing) and using the final representations of two augmented views for prediction, so we can use standard and more powerful ResNets.
We decouple the prediction task and the encoder architecture, so we do not require a context aggregation network, and our encoder can look at the images of wider spectrum of resolutions.

We use larger batch size instead of a memory bank. • Whereas maximize similarity between augmented and unaugmented copies of the same image, we apply data augmentation symmetrically to both branches of our framework (Figure ).
Most of our study for unsupervised pretraining (learning encoder network f without labels) is done using the ImageNet ILSVRC-2012 dataset .

To evaluate the learned representations, we follow the widely used linear evaluation protocol , where a linear classifier is trained on top of the frozen base network, and test accuracy is used as a proxy for representation quality.
Top-1 accuracy of models trained in different epochs (from Figure B.2), under linear evaluation and fine-tuning.

We investigated transfer learning performance on the Food-101 dataset , CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 , Birdsnap , the SUN397 scene dataset , Stanford Cars , FGVC Aircraft , the PASCAL VOC 2007 classification task , the Describable Textures Dataset (DTD) , Oxford-IIIT Pets , Caltech-101 , and Oxford 102 Flowers .

We follow the evaluation protocols in the papers introducing these datasets, i.e., we report top-1 accuracy for Food-101, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, Birdsnap, SUN397, Stanford Cars, and DTD; mean per-class accuracy for FGVC Aircraft, Oxford-IIIT Pets, Caltech-101, and Oxford 102 Flowers; and the 11-point mAP metric as defined in for PASCAL VOC 2007.
For DTD and SUN397, the dataset creators defined multiple train/test splits; we report results only for the first split.
We used the validation sets specified by the dataset creators to select hyperparameters for FGVC Aircraft, PASCAL VOC 2007, DTD, and Oxford 102 Flowers.
A simple framework for contrastive learning of visual representations.
Compared with previous work, our design choices are generally simpler.
Top-1 accuracy of different architectures under linear evaluation and fine-tuning.
We report accuracy on the test set.

downstream_contract.intended_use: Feed this JSON to a later local open-source model to write an overview.
downstream_contract.grounding_rule: The downstream generator should write from evidence_units.location.sentence and context_window, not from unsupported outside knowledge.
downstream_contract.score_warning: Scores are ranking and filtering signals, not calibrated probabilities.
